text: How does NASA know what three eye atlas has really made us? Because I'm seeing a lot of theories. Turns out they use light, because light interacts with different molecules in different ways, and so each has its own unique signature. So here's oxygen and nitrogen and hydrogen. So some telescopes have special equipment that turn observable objects into these rainbow-y light graphs that let us read the universe like a book. For example, here's Earth, as viewed by the James Webb Space Telescope. Here's water and carbon dioxide and oxygen, and there's ozone and some other gases. And what's really cool is this method can also tell us things about temperature and density and more. It's how we know that there's water vapor on a planet 700 light years away. So they're filling in the rainbow chart for three eye atlas, and early versions from the James Webb Space Telescope look like this. You can see lots of carbon dioxide and water. But researchers are now analyzing images from the comet's closest approach. So if you want more on that, follow along.
